title: Tapping for daily money panic
subtitle: A five-minute morning practice for the moment money grips you before the day starts.
excerpt: A short tapping practice for the kind of money panic that arrives at 6am: the tight chest, the bank-balance loop, the bracing before anything has happened. Five minutes, used as needed.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 1 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program to Attract Cash, Overflow & Abundance Without Hustle or Burnout (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798268793796), pp. 29. The tapping framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique (EFT), in use since the mid-1990s and derived from Roger Callahan's Thought Field Therapy.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you wake up already gripped by money: the tight chest, the bank-balance loop, the bracing before anything has happened.
practice.whenNotToUse: Skip if you're in acute crisis or actively dissociating. A grounding body practice first, then come back to this.

glossaryTerms.definition: Emotional Freedom Technique. A self-regulation practice that combines spoken phrases with light tapping on eight acupressure points on the face and upper body. Developed by Gary Craig in the mid-1990s.
The soft fleshy edge of the hand, on the side of the palm under the little finger. The starting point of an EFT round, used while reading the set-up statements.
The opening line of an EFT round, said while tapping the karate chop point. Typically of the shape "Even though I [name the feeling], I deeply and completely accept myself."

content.text: Money panic at 6am is the tight chest before you've opened your eyes. The bank-balance loop. The bracing for a day that hasn't started. This is a five-minute tapping practice for that moment: not a fix for the underlying numbers, but a way to settle the nervous system enough that you can look at the day with a steadier head.
Use it sitting up in bed, on the floor, on the loo. Anywhere you can be private for five minutes. You don't need candles, music, or perfect quiet.

attrs.title: What tapping is
attrs.body: Tapping is acupressure on eight specific points (face, collarbone, under arm, top of head) used as a self-regulation tool. It is not a treatment, a cure, or a substitute for professional support. If you're working with a healthcare or mental-health provider for anxiety, tell them you're trying this and see what they say.

content.text: The practice

Sit somewhere your feet can touch the floor. Take one slow breath in, and let it go. You'll tap each point with two or three fingers, light but firm, about five to seven taps per point. Read each line aloud if you can; under your breath or in your mind if you can't. The words don't have to be perfect. Change them if a line doesn't sit right.
Karate chop
Find the karate chop point: the soft edge of your hand, under the little finger. Tap there with two fingers of your other hand while you read these three lines. Once through is enough.
Even though I wake up worrying about money, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though I feel a knot in my stomach when I think about bills, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though it feels like there's never enough, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Tapping round
Tap each of the eight points in order, five to seven taps per point, as you read the line. Go through the whole round three times before moving to the reframe.
Eyebrow:
I wake up already stressed about money.
Side of eye:
I keep running numbers in my head.
Under eye:
I feel pressure before the day begins.
Under nose:
I'm scared of not covering everything.
Chin:
It feels like I'm always behind.
Collarbone:
I'm tired of living in this tension.
Under arm:
I feel trapped by constant worry.
Top of head:
This money panic sits heavy in my chest.
Reframe to positive
Same eight points, same order. This is where the lift starts. Tap the round three times.
Eyebrow:
I give myself permission to breathe right now.
Side of eye:
I choose calm even before anything changes.
Under eye:
I am safe in this moment.
Under nose:
I allow a sense of ease with money.
Chin:
I trust solutions can unfold step by step.
Collarbone:
I welcome support I can't yet see.
Under arm:
I open to steady income and relief.
Top of head:
I invite peace around money today.
When you've gone through the reframe three times, drop your hands. Take one slow breath, hand on your chest if it helps. Sit for a moment before you stand up.
What you might notice

A yawn or two. Tears, sometimes, on the third round. A loosening in the chest. A sudden urge to check the bank balance, or the opposite, no urge at all. The line that catches you is usually the one that wanted releasing. Sit with that line one more round if it does.
Where this practice comes from
The script is adapted from Day 1 of
MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program

(Rebecca J Page, 2025), which sits the practice inside an eighty-four-day arc from clearing money stress through to anchoring abundance. The wider tapping framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique, developed in the mid-1990s from Roger Callahan's earlier work in Thought Field Therapy. EFT has been in lay use for nearly thirty years; it sits alongside more mainstream self-regulation tools and is not a substitute for clinical care.
